body:
HK Digital Asset Radar tracks Hong Kong's evolving digital-asset regulatory regime -- what is already in force, what is still being decided, and what is officially scheduled to happen next. This page is a plain-English map to get oriented before exploring the pillar boards, the trajectory board, or the document library. Every claim on this site traces to a primary source; this page is a summary of that underlying record, not a substitute for reading the sources themselves.

Who regulates what

Four bodies do the work. The Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) is the securities regulator and the lead licensor of virtual asset trading platforms (VATPs) -- Hong Kong's licensed centralised crypto exchanges. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) is the banking and monetary authority: it licenses stablecoin issuers, supervises banks' digital-asset custody and distribution activities, and runs Hong Kong's tokenisation and digital-currency pilots. The Financial Services and the Treasury Bureau (FSTB) is the government policy bureau that sets overall digital-asset strategy (Policy Statement 2.0 and its predecessor) and, jointly with the SFC, runs the public consultations behind new licensing regimes before they become law. The Legislative Council (LegCo) is Hong Kong's legislature -- it is where a bill amending the underlying ordinances must pass before a new regime can take legal effect.

These bodies work in sequence on a typical new regime: FSTB (often jointly with the SFC) proposes an approach and consults the public; conclusions are published; a bill is drafted and introduced to LegCo; LegCo debates and passes it; the SFC or HKMA then licenses and supervises the resulting regime day to day.

The two regimes already in force

Two digital-asset regimes are fully operational today. The first is the SFC's virtual asset trading platform regime, in force since 1 June 2023. Any centralised exchange operating in Hong Kong, or actively marketing to Hong Kong investors, must hold a licence -- in practice, most platforms hold both an SFC licence (for trading security tokens) and a separate licence under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance (for operating a virtual asset exchange generally), because a token's legal classification can shift over time. The SFC's ASPIRe roadmap, published in February 2025, continues to expand this regime -- for example, permitting licensed platforms to offer staking services.

The second is the HKMA's stablecoin issuer licensing regime under the Stablecoins Ordinance, in force since 1 August 2025. Anyone issuing a stablecoin pegged to the Hong Kong dollar, or issued in Hong Kong, needs an HKMA licence, must hold at least HK$25 million in paid-up capital, and must back every issued stablecoin with reserve assets sufficient to redeem it at face value on demand. The HKMA granted its first two licences, to Anchorpoint Financial Limited and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited, on 10 April 2026; as of that licensing date neither company had yet launched a stablecoin product in the market.

What is coming next

A third regime is furthest along in the pipeline but not yet law: new licensing requirements for virtual asset dealing, custodian, advisory, and management services -- activities currently outside any Hong Kong licensing framework. The FSTB and SFC concluded their joint consultation on the dealing and custodian pieces in December 2025, and a further consultation on the advisory and management pieces closed in January 2026. Regulators have stated a target of introducing the enabling bill to the Legislative Council within 2026, though as of the most recent public update the bill had not yet been introduced, and no transitional or grandfathering arrangement is planned once it takes effect.

Alongside these licensing regimes, the government's Policy Statement 2.0 (issued June 2025) sets a broader strategic direction under what it calls the LEAP framework -- covering the incoming dealing/custodian licensing work, an expanding programme of tokenised financial products (including tokenised government bonds), and continued development of the HKMA's e-HKD digital-currency work. The SFC's enforcement activity in this space is also active and ongoing, alongside its licensing work -- including a January 2026 disciplinary action against a licensed intermediary for failures in how it distributed virtual asset products to retail clients.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas in one paragraph each. The trajectory board lists officially announced upcoming dates -- consultation deadlines, bill introductions, licensing windows -- in one place. The document library indexes every primary-source document the site's automated watcher has observed. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout. Every summary card cites the specific primary source it is drawn from.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
